_meta.author: ClerkAI Engine
_meta.notes: Fully upgraded case: dynamic responses, state-aware conditions, progression engine, consequence tracking, enhanced scoring

patient.name: Grace Taiwo
patient.basePersonality: cooperative but anxious — she is a teacher used to being in control and dislikes not understanding what is happening to her body

presentingComplaint: Progressive fatigue, nausea and ankle swelling for 2 months

hpc_onset.label: Onset & Duration
responses: I've been feeling very tired for about 2 months now. The nausea started maybe 6 weeks ago, and my legs began swelling about 3 weeks back.
It started gradually — I thought it was just stress from work. But 2 months later and I'm still not better.
The tiredness came first, then the sickness in the mornings. The swelling in my ankles started more recently — maybe 3 weeks ago.

conditions.uraemiaLevel>=3: Doctor, honestly I've been feeling like I'm dying for 2 months. The fatigue is unbearable, I can barely lift myself.
conditions.askedAfter:hpc_character: As I said, it's been about 2 months — but it's been getting much worse each week.

hpc_character.label: Character of Symptoms
responses: The fatigue is overwhelming — I can barely get through a school day. I used to be so active. The nausea is worst in the morning. I've lost about 5kg without trying.
I feel heavy all the time. Even walking to the bathroom leaves me short of breath. The nausea hits me every morning when I wake up.
It's not normal tiredness. This is different — bone-deep exhaustion. And I keep losing weight even though I'm eating whatever I can stomach.

conditions.severity=severe: Doctor, I cannot even stand at the board anymore. I had to take medical leave. Something is very wrong.
conditions.nauseaActive=true: The morning nausea is terrible. I've vomited twice this week. Food just doesn't agree with me anymore.

hpc_aggravating.label: Aggravating Factors
responses: Standing for long periods makes the leg swelling worse. Activity makes the breathlessness worse.
The nausea gets worse when I eat — especially meat or eggs. Protein seems to make everything worse.
Lying flat isn't comfortable — my breathing feels a bit better sitting up.

hpc_relieving.label: Relieving Factors
responses: Nothing really helps the fatigue. Resting the legs helps the swelling a little.
I take ibuprofen sometimes for the back pain and it helps that, but nothing helps the tiredness.
Sitting with my legs up helps a small bit. But the nausea and tiredness don't go away.

conditions.trapMedsMentioned: I've been taking ibuprofen regularly — it helps my back pain, so I take it almost every day.

sr_urinary.label: Urinary Symptoms
responses: My urine has been foamy — like it has bubbles that don't go away. And I'm passing less than usual. The colour is darker too, more like tea.
Yes, I noticed foam in my urine weeks ago but didn't think much of it. I'm not urinating as much as I should be.
The urine is foamy and dark. No burning or pain. But I feel like I'm not producing enough — the amount is definitely reduced.

conditions.ix_urinalysis:asked: Yes, those results match what I've been noticing — the foamy urine especially.
conditions.uraemiaLevel>=3: Honestly doctor, I'm barely urinating. Maybe 2 or 3 times a day, very small amounts.

sr_appetite.label: Appetite & Weight
responses: My appetite is very poor. Food doesn't appeal to me. I feel sick after eating — especially after protein like meat or fish. I've lost about 5kg in 2 months.
I force myself to eat but can barely manage. My husband notices I've lost weight. I used to love cooking but now the smell of food makes me nauseous.
Eating makes me feel worse, not better. Especially protein — eggs, meat, even beans. I've lost weight without trying.

conditions.nauseaActive=true: The morning sickness is the worst part. I barely managed breakfast today before coming here.
conditions.uraemiaLevel>=2: I've been told uraemia causes this — the build-up of waste products affects appetite. Is that what's happening to me?

sr_cardiovascular.label: Cardiovascular Symptoms
responses: I get short of breath when I climb stairs — it's gotten worse over the past month. No chest pain. My heart doesn't race.
Breathlessness on effort, yes. I sleep with 2 pillows now — I feel more comfortable that way.
I haven't had chest pain. But I do get palpitations sometimes — my heart feels like it's fluttering.

conditions.hyperkalaemiaFlag=true: I sometimes feel my heart skipping beats, doctor. Is that something to worry about?

sr_neurology.label: Neurological Symptoms
responses: I've been having headaches — almost every day for the past 2 weeks. And I find it hard to concentrate at work.
My memory isn't sharp. I write notes more than I used to. The headaches are mostly at the back of my head.
I sometimes feel restless at night — a crawling feeling in my legs. My husband says I kick a lot in my sleep.

conditions.bpControl=uncontrolled: The headaches are bad, especially in the morning — very severe, at the back of my head.

sr_skin.label: Skin Symptoms
responses: Yes — I've been itching all over for about a month. My skin feels dry and I scratch especially at night.
The itching is unbearable at times — mostly on my back and arms. Nothing seems to relieve it.
I scratch without even realising it. My skin looks different too — slightly yellowish.

conditions.uraemiaLevel>=2: The itching started when everything else got worse. I've scratched my skin raw in some places.

pmh_general.label: Past Medical History
responses: I have type 2 diabetes for 10 years and high blood pressure for 8 years. I was told 2 years ago that my kidneys were 'a bit affected' but I didn't follow up — I thought they meant it was minor.
Diabetes and hypertension — both for many years. My previous doctor mentioned something about the kidneys once but I was busy and didn't go back.
High blood pressure and sugar diabetes. I've had both for a long time. I was also told my kidneys showed something on a test 2 years ago.

conditions.ix_lft:asked: Yes, those kidney numbers — the doctor mentioned something similar before but not this bad.
conditions.askedAfter:meds_general: Yes, and I've been on medication for both the blood pressure and the diabetes for years.

pmh_family.label: Family History
responses: My mother had high blood pressure and died from kidney problems — but we weren't told the exact diagnosis.
Hypertension runs in my family. My father and one sister also have it. I don't know of any kidney disease.
My mother's health was poor for years before she died. The doctors said it was 'kidney failure' but I was young and didn't understand.

pmh_surgical.label: Surgical History
responses: I had a Caesarean section 15 years ago for my last child. No other operations.
Only surgery was a CS — no complications. No other procedures.
Just the CS years ago. Nothing else surgically.

social_history.label: Social History
responses: I'm a secondary school teacher. Married, 3 children. I don't smoke or drink alcohol. We have clean water at home. I cook with firewood sometimes.
Teacher, married. No alcohol, no tobacco. I'm under a lot of stress at work — large class sizes and I take on extra duties.
I live with my husband and youngest child. No smoking. I sometimes use local herbs — my neighbour recommended something for energy.

meds_general.label: Medications
responses: Metformin 1g twice a day, Lisinopril 10mg, and Amlodipine 5mg. And — I take ibuprofen regularly for back pain. Sometimes every day.
The doctor gave me Metformin for the diabetes and Lisinopril for the blood pressure. Also Amlodipine. I buy ibuprofen from the pharmacy for my back — it's been years I've been taking it.
Metformin, Lisinopril, Amlodipine — those are my prescribed ones. I also take pain killers. Ibuprofen mostly. Almost daily.

conditions.trapTriggered:nsaid: Yes, I already told you — ibuprofen. Is that a problem? The pharmacist never mentioned anything.

meds_general.clinicalNote: Contains 2 trap medications: metformin (lactic acidosis risk in CKD eGFR<30) and ibuprofen (nephrotoxic NSAID). Learner must identify and address both.

allergies.label: Allergies
responses: No known drug allergies. I've taken penicillin before without problems.
None that I know of. No reactions to any medication.
No allergies. I tolerate all medications I've been given.

exam_general.label: General Examination
responses: General: Patient is pale — conjunctival pallor present. There is a subtle uraemic tinge (yellowish-brown) to the skin. Scratch marks visible on arms and back (pruritus). BP 176/104 mmHg. Pulse 88 bpm regular. RR 18/min. SpO2 97% on air. Bilateral pitting oedema 2+ to ankles. Bilateral periorbital puffiness noted.
On examination: Pallor +2, uraemic fetor on breath. Skin: excoriation marks, dry, slightly discoloured. Vitals: BP 176/104, HR 88 bpm, Temp 36.8°C. Pitting oedema bilaterally to mid-shin. Puffy eyes.

conditions.uraemiaLevel>=3: ⚠️ WORSENING: Patient now appears more drowsy. GCS 14/15. Uraemic fetor pronounced. BP now 192/110. Oedema 3+ to knees.
conditions.bpControl=uncontrolled: BP remains severely elevated at 176/104 despite antihypertensive therapy — indicates target organ damage.

exam_cardiovascular.label: Cardiovascular Examination
responses: CVS: JVP elevated 4cm above sternal angle. Apex beat displaced to 6th intercostal space, anterior axillary line (cardiomegaly). S1 S2 heard with a soft S3 gallop. No significant murmurs. Peripheral pulses present.
Cardiovascular: Raised JVP. Displaced apex beat — cardiomegaly likely. Soft third heart sound. Tachycardia not present. Peripheral oedema bilateral.

conditions.hyperkalaemiaFlag=true: ⚠️ ALERT: Monitor for arrhythmia. Potassium 6.1 mmol/L — cardiac membrane at risk.

exam_respiratory.label: Respiratory Examination
responses: Respiratory: Mild bibasal fine crepitations — likely fluid overload. No pleural effusion clinically. RR 18/min. SpO2 97%.
Chest: Bilateral basal crackles. No wheeze. Resonant percussion. Mild pulmonary congestion pattern.

conditions.oedemaGrade>=3: Significant bilateral crackles extending to mid-zones. Consider pulmonary oedema.

exam_abdomen.label: Abdominal Examination
responses: Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender in loin regions bilaterally. No renal masses palpable. No renal bruit on auscultation. Bladder not palpable. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No ascites (shifting dullness absent).
Abdominal exam: No organomegaly. No palpable kidneys — bilaterally. No renal angle tenderness. No ascites. Bladder not palpable.

exam_neuro.label: Neurological Examination
responses: Neuro: Alert and oriented to time, place, person. GCS 15/15. Mild asterixis (metabolic flap) on sustained wrist extension — consistent with uraemic encephalopathy early stage.
Neurological: Conscious and oriented. Mild asterixis present bilaterally. Peripheral neuropathy signs: reduced vibration sense in feet bilaterally.

conditions.uraemiaLevel>=3: ⚠️ PROGRESSION: GCS now 13/15. Marked asterixis. Confusion — patient is not oriented to time. Uraemic encephalopathy advancing.

ix_urinalysis.label: Urinalysis / Dipstick
responses:
Urinalysis:
• Protein: 3+ (significant proteinuria)
• Blood: 2+ (haematuria)
• Glucose: 2+ (glycosuria — uncontrolled DM)
• Nitrites: Negative
• Leucocytes: Negative

Spot ACR: 420 mg/mmol — severely increased albuminuria (CKD diagnostic criterion exceeded)

Dipstick urine:
• Heavy proteinuria (3+)
• Blood 2+
• Glucose 2+
• No infection markers

ACR 420 mg/mmol — severely elevated. This level indicates significant glomerular damage.

conditions.ix_lft:asked: With ACR 420 and creatinine this elevated, the clinical picture is consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

ix_fbc.label: Full Blood Count
responses:
FBC:
• Hb: 8.2 g/dL ↓ — normocytic normochromic anaemia (anaemia of CKD — reduced EPO production)
• MCV: 84 fL (normocytic)
• WBC: 8.4 × 10⁹/L — normal
• Neutrophils: 5.2 × 10⁹/L — normal
• Platelets: 280 × 10⁹/L — normal

Comment: Anaemia consistent with reduced erythropoietin in CKD. No evidence of haemolysis or iron deficiency pattern.

Blood count:
• Anaemia: Hb 8.2 — normocytic (EPO deficiency)
• WBC and platelets: within normal limits
• No thrombocytopenia to suggest HELLP or TTP

ix_lft.label: Renal Function Tests (U&E, Creatinine, eGFR)
responses:
Renal Function:
• Creatinine: 486 μmol/L ↑↑ (Normal: 45–90)
• eGFR: 11 mL/min/1.73m² → CKD Stage 5 (ESRD)
• Urea: 28 mmol/L ↑↑ (uraemia)
• Potassium: 6.1 mmol/L ↑↑ ← HYPERKALAEMIA — arrhythmia risk
• Sodium: 134 mmol/L (mild hyponatraemia)
• Bicarbonate: 14 mmol/L ↓ (metabolic acidosis — high anion gap)
• Phosphate: 2.1 mmol/L ↑ (hyperphosphataemia)
• Calcium: 1.9 mmol/L ↓ (hypocalcaemia — renal osteodystrophy)

⚠️ CRITICAL: K⁺ 6.1 requires urgent management — ECG now.

U&E / Creatinine:
• eGFR 11 — end-stage territory
• Cr 486 — severely elevated
• K⁺ 6.1 — dangerously high, risk of fatal arrhythmia
• Bicarb 14 — metabolic acidosis
• Urea 28 — uraemia confirmed

conditions.complications:miss_hyperkalaemia: ⛔ COMPLICATION TRIGGERED: Hyperkalaemia (K 6.1) was not acted upon in time. See progression event.
conditions.state:ix_lft_acknowledged: Given K 6.1, what is your immediate management plan for the hyperkalaemia?

ix_lft_liver.label: Liver Function Tests
responses:
LFTs:
• Total Bilirubin: 14 μmol/L (normal)
• ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
• AST: 32 U/L (normal)
• ALP: 145 U/L ↑ (mild elevation — renal osteodystrophy related)
• Albumin: 28 g/L ↓ (hypoalbuminaemia — nephrotic losses + poor nutrition)
• Total Protein: 52 g/L ↓
Liver function: No hepatocellular damage. Mildly elevated ALP (bone related). Low albumin consistent with proteinuria and uraemia.

ix_rbs.label: Random Blood Sugar / HbA1c
responses:
RBS: 11.4 mmol/L (poorly controlled diabetes)
HbA1c: 9.8% — very poor glycaemic control over 3 months

Note: With eGFR 11, HbA1c may be falsely low due to shortened RBC lifespan. Fructosamine may be more accurate.

Blood glucose: Elevated at 11.4 mmol/L
HbA1c: 9.8% — suboptimal control
In advanced CKD, HbA1c reliability is reduced. Consider fructosamine.

ix_ecg.label: ECG
responses:
ECG:
• Rate: 88 bpm
• Rhythm: Sinus rhythm
• PR interval: 0.22s (borderline prolonged)
• QRS: 0.10s
• T waves: TALL PEAKED T waves in V1–V4 ← HYPERKALAEMIA SIGN
• No ST changes

⚠️ Peaked T waves = impending cardiac toxicity from K⁺ 6.1. URGENT MANAGEMENT REQUIRED.
ECG findings: Sinus rhythm 88bpm. Peaked T waves across precordial leads — classical hyperkalaemia pattern. Borderline PR prolongation. No VT/VF at present — but this may change without treatment.

conditions.complications:miss_hyperkalaemia: ⛔ ECG now shows widened QRS — progression to cardiac toxicity. Patient requires emergency intervention.

ix_ultrasound.label: Renal Ultrasound
responses:
Renal USS:
• Right kidney: 8.2 cm (↓ — normal >9 cm)
• Left kidney: 8.0 cm (↓)
• Echogenicity: Increased bilaterally — echogenic cortex
• Corticomedullary differentiation: Reduced
• Cortical thickness: Thinned bilaterally
• No hydronephrosis
• No obstruction
• No renal masses or calculi

→ Bilateral small echogenic kidneys = hallmark of CHRONIC kidney disease (not acute). This confirms chronicity.

USS Kidneys:
• Both kidneys shrunken — 8.0–8.2 cm
• Increased echogenicity
• Cortical thinning
• No obstruction

This pattern is diagnostic of chronic parenchymal disease — irreversible.

ix_urine_mc_s.label: Urine M/C/S
responses:
Urine M/C/S:
• No growth after 48 hours
• No significant bacteriuria
• RBCs on microscopy (haematuria — glomerular origin, dysmorphic red cells)
• Granular casts present — consistent with tubular damage
• No WBC casts

ix_bone_profile.label: Bone Profile / PTH
responses:
Bone profile:
• Calcium: 1.9 mmol/L ↓ (hypocalcaemia)
• Phosphate: 2.1 mmol/L ↑ (hyperphosphataemia)
• PTH: 380 pg/mL ↑↑ (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
• Vitamin D: 12 ng/mL ↓ (deficiency — reduced renal activation)

Comment: Consistent with CKD-MBD (Mineral Bone Disorder)

trapActions.explanation: ⛔ NSAIDs are nephrotoxic and absolutely contraindicated in CKD. They reduce renal blood flow via prostaglandin inhibition, further impairing an already critically reduced eGFR. This could precipitate acute-on-chronic kidney failure. Deducted −20 pts.
trapActions.correctAction: Stop ibuprofen immediately. Switch to paracetamol (acetaminophen) for analgesia — it is renally safe. Opioids can be used cautiously at reduced doses if needed.

trapActions.explanation: ⚠️ Metformin must be withheld when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m². With eGFR of 11, there is serious risk of metformin accumulation and potentially fatal lactic acidosis. Deducted −15 pts.
trapActions.correctAction: Stop metformin. Switch glycaemic management to insulin (dose-adjusted for eGFR) or linagliptin (a DPP-4 inhibitor safe in CKD). Monitor glucose closely.

trapActions.explanation: ⚠️ ACE inhibitors increase potassium. With K⁺ already at 6.1 mmol/L, increasing the ACE inhibitor dose risks worsening hyperkalaemia. The existing ACE inhibitor is protective for the kidneys long-term, but dose escalation here is dangerous. Deducted −10 pts.

⚠️ IV contrast is relatively contraindicated in advanced CKD (eGFR <30). Risk of contrast-induced nephropathy which can precipitate dialysis. If CT is necessary, use non-contrast CT or pre-hydrate with IV normal saline and minimise contrast volume. Deducted −15 pts.

timeEvents.event: 🔔 SYSTEM: Lab nurse calls — 'Doctor, have you seen the potassium result? It's 6.1.'
⚠️ PROGRESSION: Patient becoming drowsy. GCS drops to 13/15. Uraemic encephalopathy worsening. Asterixis more pronounced.
🚨 CRITICAL EVENT: Monitor alarms. Patient has palpitations, looks pale and sweaty. Telemetry shows peaked T waves progressing to widened QRS.
⏰ Time limit approaching. Summarise your assessment and initial management plan.

complications.event: 🚨 CARDIAC ARRHYTHMIA: K⁺ 6.1 unmanaged → VT/VF risk. Patient clutches chest. Get crash cart.
complications.teachingPoint: Hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >6.0) in CKD requires immediate ECG and urgent management: IV calcium gluconate (cardioprotection), insulin-dextrose (shifts K into cells), consider dialysis.

complications.event: ⛔ ACUTE ON CHRONIC KI: NSAIDs have further reduced renal perfusion. Creatinine now 620 μmol/L — rising. Patient has decreased urine output. Urgent nephrology review required.
complications.teachingPoint: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation. In CKD, the kidney depends on this mechanism to maintain GFR. NSAIDs = acute-on-chronic kidney injury.

complications.event: ⚠️ LACTIC ACIDOSIS RISK: Metformin continued in eGFR 11. Lactate rising. Patient developing nausea, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing.
complications.teachingPoint: Metformin is cleared renally. In eGFR <30, it accumulates, inhibiting mitochondrial complex I → lactic acidosis. Must be stopped.

managementChecklist._comment: Optional: learner can propose management. Engine scores against this checklist.

shortTerm.action: Low protein, low potassium, low phosphate diet + renal dietitian referral
Urgent nephrology referral for dialysis planning (eGFR 11)

longTerm.action: Treat CKD-MBD: phosphate binders, vitamin D supplementation, PTH monitoring
Optimise glycaemic control with renally-safe agents (linagliptin / insulin)

teachingPoints: CKD causes anaemia via reduced EPO production — not iron deficiency. Normocytic pattern is key.
Bilateral small echogenic kidneys on USS = chronicity confirmed — distinguishes CKD from AKI.
Hyperkalaemia in CKD (K⁺ >6.0) is a medical emergency: ECG, calcium gluconate, insulin-dextrose.
NSAIDs are absolutely contraindicated in CKD — prostaglandin inhibition reduces GFR further.
Metformin must be stopped when eGFR <30 due to lactic acidosis risk.
ACE inhibitors (Lisinopril) are reno-protective in diabetic nephropathy — continue but monitor K⁺.
Diabetic nephropathy + hypertension for >10 years → most likely aetiology in this case.
eGFR 11 = CKD Stage 5 (ESRD territory) — urgent dialysis planning required.
Metabolic acidosis in CKD: bicarbonate supplementation may slow disease progression.
